race.name: AIDS/LifeCycle
race.tagline: 545 miles. 7 days. 1 mission. The most emotionally powerful cycling event ever created. San Francisco to Los Angeles. $300 million raised. Where the cycling community rides to end AIDS.

vitals.location: San Francisco to Los Angeles, California, USA
vitals.location_badge: SAN FRANCISCO TO LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA
vitals.date_specific: 2025 was the final ride (June 1-7, 2025). Successor event: Cycle to Zero (3-day ride, May 29-31, 2026, SFAF only)
vitals.registration: Online registration. $125 registration fee + $3,500 minimum fundraising commitment. Roadie (volunteer) registration has no fundraising minimum. All funds benefit San Francisco AIDS Foundation and Los Angeles LGBT Center.
vitals.entry_fee: $125 registration fee + $3,500 minimum fundraising commitment. Heavy Hitter levels available for higher fundraising goals.
vitals.feed_zones: Rest stops every 15-20 miles with water, electrolyte solutions, and snacks. Full lunch stop each day (Boudin catering). Medical support at every rest stop. Chiropractic and sports medicine at lunch. 20-30 sweep vehicles on course. SAG buses available for transport to camp.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous. SAG buses and sweep vehicles support all riders. No one is left behind. This is a ride, not a race.
route_options: Full ride: 545 miles over 7 days (San Francisco to Los Angeles)
Single-day options available on some days (join for one day as a registered participant)

climate.primary: California coastal — fog, sun, inland heat, and coastal wind across 7 days and 545 miles
climate.description: Seven days on the California coast in June means experiencing nearly every microclimate the state offers. San Francisco departs in classic morning fog and chill (55-60F). Day 1 over Skyline can be windy and cool. Central Coast days through King City and Paso Robles bring inland heat (85-95F) with dry air and intense sun. Coastal sections near Santa Barbara and Ventura moderate back to 65-75F with ocean breezes. Wind is a constant companion, particularly afternoon headwinds along the coast. Evening camp temperatures drop to 50-55F, requiring warm layers for sleeping.
challenges: Temperature swings from 50F morning fog to 95F inland heat within a single day
Persistent coastal headwinds, particularly in afternoon riding
Sun exposure across 7 days of riding — cumulative sunburn and dehydration risk
Inland heat through Paso Robles and Santa Maria valleys
Cool, windy camps requiring warm sleeping gear despite daytime heat

terrain.primary: Paved California roads from San Francisco to Los Angeles
terrain.surface: All paved roads. A mix of California state highways, county roads, Pacific Coast Highway sections, and rural farm roads. Day 1 features the hardest climbing — Skyline Boulevard out of San Francisco with 4,700+ feet of elevation gain. Day 3 includes the notorious Quadbuster (15% grade) and rolling terrain into Paso Robles. Day 4 features the Evil Twins — two (or three) steep peaks marking the halfway point. Days 5-7 include Gaviota Pass and coastal riding into Ventura and Santa Monica.
features: Day 1: Skyline Boulevard — 4,700+ feet of climbing out of San Francisco, the ride's hardest day
Day 3: Quadbuster — a 15% grade hill that lives up to its name
Day 4: Evil Twins — two steep peaks marking the emotional and physical halfway point
Day 6: Gaviota Pass — 1,100 feet of climbing before dropping to the Santa Barbara coast
Day 7: Pacific Coast Highway into Santa Monica — the triumphant finish along the ocean
Rest stops every 15-20 miles — you are never far from support

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 71. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 67.

course_description.character: Seven days of California coastal beauty and emotional intensity. The route traverses the full diversity of the state's Central Coast — from San Francisco fog through redwood forests, over mountain ridges, through agricultural valleys, past wine country, over coastal passes, and along the Pacific Coast Highway to a Santa Monica finish. The terrain is varied but always paved, with significant climbing concentrated on Days 1, 3, 4, and 6. The real challenge is cumulative: 545 miles over 7 consecutive days in the saddle, compounded by camping, emotional weight, and California's variable June weather.
course_description.signature_challenge: The cumulative toll of 545 miles over 7 days. No single day is impossible, but the relentless accumulation of miles, climbing, weather, camping, and emotional weight creates an endurance challenge that transforms riders. The body adapts; the spirit grows. By Day 7, riding into Santa Monica feels like crossing into a different version of your life.

logistics.airport: SFO (San Francisco International) for start. LAX (Los Angeles International) for finish. Many participants fly one direction and have bikes shipped.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Camp cities are provided each night at designated locations: Harvey West Park (Santa Cruz), San Lorenzo County Park (King City), Mid-State Fairgrounds (Paso Robles), Preisker Park (Santa Maria), River Bend Park (Lompoc), San Buenaventura State Beach (Ventura). Tents provided or bring your own. Some veterans self-support in hotels along the route. Gear trucks transport personal bags between camps.
logistics.food: All meals included. Breakfast and dinner at camp. Lunch provided by Boudin at midday stops. Rest stop snacks and hydration every 15-20 miles. Vegetarian and omnivorous options available. You will not go hungry.

history.origin_story: The California AIDS Ride launched in 1994 as a week-long cycling event from San Francisco to Los Angeles to raise money and awareness for HIV/AIDS services. After Pallotta Teamworks faced controversy over overhead costs, the event was restructured in 2002 as AIDS/LifeCycle, directly co-produced by San Francisco AIDS Foundation and Los Angeles LGBT Center. Over 30 years, the event raised more than $300 million and became the largest HIV/AIDS fundraiser in the world. In September 2024, the organizations announced that the 2025 ride would be the final AIDS/LifeCycle, citing rising production costs and declining post-pandemic participation. The final ride on June 1-7, 2025 drew 2,067 cyclists and 750+ roadies. SFAF launched Cycle to Zero as a 3-day successor event for 2026.
notable_moments: 1994: California AIDS Ride launches — first major multi-day cycling fundraiser for HIV/AIDS
2002: Restructured as AIDS/LifeCycle under direct nonprofit co-production
Over 30 years: $300 million+ raised for HIV/AIDS services
Peak participation: approximately 3,000 cyclists in banner years
2024: Announcement that 2025 would be the final ride
2025: Final ride — 2,067 cyclists and 750+ roadies complete the last 545 miles
2026: Cycle to Zero launches as 3-day SFAF successor event
history.reputation: The most culturally significant cycling event in American history. Not just a ride but a movement — a 30-year tradition of LGBTQ+ community, activism, remembrance, and defiance.

biased_opinion.summary: AIDS/LifeCycle is not a race. It is not a gran fondo. It is not a charity ride in the way most people understand charity rides. It is 545 miles of collective grief, joy, defiance, and love, pedaled down the California coast by thousands of people who refuse to let a disease define or defeat their community. You will climb the Quadbuster with disco music blasting. You will cry at camp listening to names of the dead read aloud. You will finish in Santa Monica having raised thousands of dollars and having been fundamentally changed by the experience. Over 30 years, this event raised more than $300 million and created a community that transcends cycling. The 2025 final ride ended an era — but the legacy is permanent.
strengths: The community: Nothing in cycling compares. Year-round training, team fundraising, and shared purpose create bonds that last decades. Veterans return 10, 15, 20 times.
The emotional depth: Memorial services, riders wearing photos of lost loved ones, names read aloud at camp. This is cycling as an act of remembrance and resistance.
The logistics: A traveling camp city for 3,000+ people with full meals, showers, medical, bike tech, and entertainment — moved 545 miles in 7 days by 600+ volunteers.
The California coast: 545 miles from San Francisco's fog to Santa Monica's sun. Redwoods, farmland, vineyards, ocean cliffs, PCH. One of the most beautiful cycling routes in America.
weaknesses: The fundraising minimum: $3,500 is a significant barrier. Many potential riders never register because the financial commitment feels insurmountable.

It ended: The 2025 ride was the final AIDS/LifeCycle. Rising production costs and declining post-pandemic ridership led to the announcement in September 2024. SFAF launched Cycle to Zero as a 3-day successor.
Camping for 7 nights: Not everyone thrives sleeping on the ground for a week. Some veterans eventually self-support in hotels along the route.
No competitive element: If you need a timing chip and a finish time to feel motivated, this event will not give you that. The value is entirely experiential and emotional.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you were fortunate enough to ride AIDS/LifeCycle during its 30-year run, you experienced something that no other cycling event has ever replicated — and likely never will. The successor Cycle to Zero carries the spirit forward in a 3-day format, but the 545 was singular. It was cycling at its most human.

final_verdict.one_liner: The most emotionally powerful cycling event ever created. 545 miles of purpose, community, and remembrance down the California coast.
final_verdict.should_you_race: AIDS/LifeCycle ended in 2025 after 30 years and $300 million raised. If you rode it, you know what it meant. If you never did, the successor event Cycle to Zero (3-day, SFAF-produced) launches in May 2026. The 545-mile journey from San Francisco to Los Angeles is gone, but the community and mission endure. This was never about the miles — it was about why you rode them.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want the charity ride experience: Pan-Mass Challenge (177 miles, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, $1B+ raised). If you want a multi-day California ride: Cycle to Zero (3-day successor, May 2026, SFAF). If you want a long-distance community experience: RAGBRAI (week-long ride across Iowa, ~400 miles). If you want cycling with deep purpose: Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle (Ireland, 112 miles, charity-focused).

videos.title: ALC x 2022 - Aids LifeCycle - 545mi SF to LA
videos.description: From June 5-11 we rode 545mi from San Francisco to Los Angeles in support of the San Francisco Aids Foundation and the Los Angeles LGBT Center.

videos.title: (360˚ Video) Day 1 Ride Out
videos.description:
Watch riders take off in 360˚ at the start of AIDS Lifecycle 2016! Experience a minute of ALC 2016 through this 360˚ video by moving around your android phone or scrolling through it on your computer.

Each year, over 3,000 participants in AIDS/LifeCycle make an epic journey down the coast of California in the 545-mile, 7-day bike ride from San Francisco to Los Angeles. In its history, spanning more than a decade, over $130 million dollars has been raised for the critical HIV/AIDS services provided by San Francisco AIDS Foundation and the Los Angeles LGBT Center. 

Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/AIDSLifeCycle
Instagram: http://instagram.com/aidslifecycle
Twitter: https://twitter.com/aidslifecycle

About AIDS/LifeCycle: http://tinyurl.com/pazk8t8
Life On Event: http://tinyurl.com/owhasep
Life In Camp: http://tinyurl.com/ny54rqw
Training & Safety: http://tinyurl.com/k3tn8fk
Fundraising: http://tinyurl.com/luohn9q
Volunteering as a Roadie: http://tinyurl.com/lpnoqbk

Benefiting Organizations:
Los Angeles LGBT Center: http://www.lalgbtcenter.org/
SF AIDS Foundation: http://www.sfaf.org/

AIDS/LifeCycle benefits, and is jointly produced by, San Francisco AIDS Foundation and Los Angeles LGBT Center each of which is a nonprofit, public benefit corporation recognized as tax exempt under IRS Code Section 501(c)(3). Donations to AIDS/LifeCycle are deductible for income tax purposes, to the extent permitted by law.

©2016 AIDS/LifeCycle
Video produced by TrimTab Media: http://trimtabmedia.tv

videos.title: ALC2: From SF to LA with Love Part 2 of 3
videos.description: In the Summer of 2003, I embarked on a very special journey as a roadie for AIDS/LifeCycle 2.  During my free time from my stage set-up duties, I shot footage of the ride and interviewed various persons (mostly friends). There were some surprises, including a serious injury on the first day, some special bonds and a lot of heartfelt laughter and sharing of stories. Each person tells why they decided to ride in their own words. This documentary was shown at the 2004 Frameline SF Gay + Lesbian Film Festival for an audience of 1,000.

videos.curation_reason: Documentary-style with detailed rider interviews about training, physical challenges, and personal motivation stories.

videos.title: Gus Kenworthy's AIDS/LifeCycle Journey
videos.description:
Olympic skier Gus Kenworthy set his sights on not only completing AIDS/LifeCycle but challenging himself to raise more money & awareness than ever before. This year marks the 15th anniversary of Cannondale’s partnership with AIDS/LifeCycle. Over this time, we’ve helped over 30,000 riders complete their 545-mile journey from San Francisco to Los Angeles and witnessed almost $200 Million fundraised by these amazing riders.
To learn more about Gus's journey, click here: http://www.tofighthiv.org/site/TR?px=3370255&fr_id=2110&pg=personal
videos.transcript:
this ride for me is just a way to give
back I feel like I've been so fortunate
with the time that I was born as a gay
man the fact that I've been able to be
out and proud and still have a career
and still be able to compete and thrive
I think it's important to remind people
and important to break down the stigma
an important to try and raise money for
the people that are currently HIV
positive and try and prevent anybody
else from becoming HIV positive
I think that being an Olympic athlete I
have a mental toughness and I have an
ability to focus on a task and figure
out how to accomplish it and I think
that that's definitely benefiting me
here I'm just setting small goals on the
way to the big goal to get to the finish
line
[Music]
[Applause]
videos.curation_reason: Features Olympic athlete's first-person perspective with mental toughness insights applicable to the 545-mile challenge.

quotes.text: I think that being an Olympic athlete I have a mental toughness and I have an ability to focus on a task and figure out how to accomplish it and I think that that's definitely benefiting me here I'm just setting small goals on the way to the big goal to get to the finish line.

You look around you see all the people everyone's got a good Community Spirit they bust their ass all day they ride 100 miles and take back help each other set their tents up and it's a great experience.

race_day_tips.text: Set small goals on the way to the big goal to get to the finish line. Use mental toughness and focus on accomplishing tasks step by step.
Training rides are essential - doing training rides is like being a day on the actual ride. Don't be afraid to join group training rides.

additional_quotes.text: I remember my first training ride I thought I was going to die going up a hill just because I'd been smoking. I quit a month before and I was just like my first ride I was too scared to go on any training rides with anyone cuz I was scared I would always be last so I did the training on my own and I only did five weeks of training.

You look around you see all the people everyone's got a good Community Spirit they bust their ass all day they ride 100 miles and take back help each other set their tents up and it's a great experience.

rider_intel.search_text: AIDS/LifeCycle is a 545-mile charity ride from San Francisco to Los Angeles with 25,000 feet of elevation gain over multiple days. Riders describe it as mentally, physically, and spiritually challenging, with daily distances around 100 miles. The event features camping logistics where participants help each other set up tents after long riding days. Training is crucial - riders recommend 4 months of preparation including group training rides, as these simulate actual ride conditions. The event attracts participants seeking personal challenges while raising money for HIV/AIDS causes. Riders emphasize the strong community spirit and supportive atmosphere throughout the multi-day journey. Mental toughness and setting incremental goals are key strategies for completing this demanding endurance event.
